stem: In a university admissions study comparing Department A vs. Department B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher acceptance rate (70.5% vs 54.3%). However, when stratified by applicant pool competitiveness: In less competitive applicants (n_X=20, n_Y=150): Treatment X achieves 84% vs Treatment Y's 79%. In highly competitive applicants (n_X=120, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 49% vs Treatment Y's 40%. The true population proportion of less competitive applicants is uncertain, estimated between 40% and 54%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to highly competitive applicants (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.068, 0.074], point estimate: 0.071. Treatment X is genuinely better by 7.1 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=54.3%, Y=70.5% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In less competitive applicants: X=84% > Y=79%. In highly competitive applicants: X=49% > Y=40%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Department A is applied more to highly competitive applicants (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
